exemplar.title: To what extent do media outlets promote Palestinians as the aggressors in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict to Western audiences?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do media outlets promote Palestinians as the aggressors in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict to Western audiences?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: The investigation is framed with clear purpose, linking each case study back to the research question to maintain consistent focus.
The research question is explicitly stated and revisited throughout, giving a strong sense of direction to the analysis.
The methodology is appropriate and well-justified, with clear evidence of informed source selection and an effective range of media artefacts.
The title and introduction succinctly communicate the investigationâs scope and rationale, engaging the reader from the outset.
weaknesses: The scope remains somewhat broad (e.g. undefined time period and selection criteria for âmedia outletsâ), which occasionally leads to descriptive passages rather than focused enquiry.
Only three case studies are used, limiting representativeness; selection and exclusion criteria are not fully transparent.
Analytical procedures (e.g. steps in the rhetorical analysis) are under-explained, reducing replicability.
Some historical background (e.g. Balfour Declaration overview) lacks direct linkage to the specific media analysis, diluting focus.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student demonstrates an accurate and effective framing of the topic and a generally complete methodology, there are minor gaps in the operationalisation of scope and analytical procedures. On balance, the work sits in the 5â6 band, meriting 5 of 6.

strengths: Demonstrates excellent contextual knowledge by integrating primary and secondary sources on historical and contemporary media events.
Sources are used coherently to substantiate claims about framing, with correct Chicago-style citations and an extensive bibliography.
Terminology (e.g. hyperbole, diction, passive/active voice, appeals to authority) is applied accurately and consistently throughout the analysis.
Visual evidence (screenshots, comparative images) is woven into the narrative to enhance understanding and confirm analytical points.
weaknesses: A few historical assertions go unchallenged and would benefit from theoretical or empirical support.
Occasional drift into evaluative language (âstrategically manipulativeâ) without grounding in media-bias frameworks weakens academic rigor.
Reliance on a single social media post limits evidentiary breadth; additional primary outlets or scholarly viewpoints would strengthen source diversity.
Some citations (e.g. social media timestamps) lack full publication details required for precise verification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows excellent knowledge and effective use of sources and terminology, with only minor unevenness. This performance aligns with the upper end of the 5â6 band, justifying a score of 5 of 6.

strengths: Employs a wide, current and directly relevant body of primary and secondary sources consistently tied back to the research question.
Analysis is text-specific and persuasive, unpacking lexical framing (e.g. âEXCLUSIVE,â âRAWâ) and grammatical choices to reveal ideological effects.
Arguments progress logically from micro-level rhetorical features to macro-level implications, demonstrating a clear thread through the essay.
Conclusions are well supported by evidence, and the student reflects on broader diplomatic and ethical implications, adding depth to evaluation.
weaknesses: Counter-arguments and alternative readings are under-explored, leading to a somewhat one-sided critique.
Some assertions (e.g. uniform Palestinian support for Hamas) overgeneralise and lack demographic or quantitative substantiation.
Quantitative measures (e.g. frequency of mistranslation errors) are absent, weakening claims of systemic bias.
Critical reflection on the studentâs own methodological limitations is limited; more self-evaluation would enhance balance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates excellent research relevance, robust analysis and a coherent evaluative discussion. Minor lapses in counter-argument inclusion and quantitative support prevent a perfect 12, placing it at the lower end of the 10â12 band. A score of 10 of 12 is therefore justified.

strengths: All required structural elements (title page, table of contents, clearly labelled sections, bibliography) are present and correctly organised.
Headings and subheadings guide the reader through the argument, maintaining clear flow and coherence.
Consistent use of Chicago full-note referencing, page numbering, and figure captions upholds professional presentation standards.
Minor formatting errors (e.g. HTML tags) do not impede readability.
weaknesses: HTML artefacts (<b> tags) appear in the table of contents, detracting from polish.
Some long URLs are not truncated or formatted, affecting visual neatness.
Occasional duplicated bibliography entries and liberal use of âIbid.â make navigation less user-friendly.
A few figure captions lack precise source attribution, reducing clarity of evidence sourcing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is highly competent, meeting all IB structural and formatting requirements with only superficial errors. This places the work firmly in the 3â4 band and warrants the maximum of 4 of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Identifiable but not exhaustive method.**  The student explains choosing three prominent Western media texts (IDF tweet, BBC video, two Economist articles) and applies rhetorical analysis, citing Chicago-style sources.  The method is generally appropriate for Language & Literature EE expectations.  

**Reasoned selection, yet narrow corpus.**  While each case is justified, the range is limited (only three examples, all from late-2023).  No transparent criteria for selecting or excluding other outlets, limiting representativeness; data-gathering procedures (e.g., how view counts or audience reception were analysed) are not detailed.  

**Mostly complete but with identifiable gaps.**  Documentation and source handling are sound, yet the restricted sample and limited explanation of analytical framework prevent demonstration of a fully âcomplete and effectiveâ methodology, situating the work in the 3-4 band with a score of 4.

**Generally correct formatting.**  Word count, page count and candidate details are provided; Chicago full-note referencing is used consistently; page numbers and figure captions are included.  

**Minor but noticeable lapses.**  Table of contents shows HTML artefacts, some long URLs are not truncated, and a few duplicated bibliography entries appear.  Figures occasionally lack precise source attribution (only general caption).  

**Meets requirements with small errors.**  These inconsistencies lower professional polish but do not breach IB layout rules.  Placement at the lower end of the 3-4 band, score 3, is appropriate.

**Wide range of contemporary, directly relevant sources.**  The corpus includes primary media texts from X/Twitter, BBC and The Economist, supplemented by over 30 secondary references spanning academic articles, news agencies and think-tank material.  

**Relevance to the RQ is consistently evident.**  All selected items pertain to Western portrayals of the conflict and feed directly into the studentâs argumentative trajectory.  The sources are properly cited, and most are current (2023-24), enabling up-to-date analysis.  

**Minor weaknesses prevent top-band.**  The selection under-represents sources challenging the essayâs thesis and lacks explicit methodological justification for inclusion/exclusion.  Nevertheless, breadth, currency and pertinence fit comfortably in the 7-9 band, with a score of 8.

**Clear, purposeful framing of the investigation.**  The title succinctly signals both the broad field (media representation) and the specific focus (Palestinians as aggressors), while the opening paragraphs articulate the rationale and personal motivation.  

**Sustained relevance to research focus.**  Throughout the essay the student repeatedly links discussion back to the guiding theme of Western media portraying Palestinians as aggressors; all three case studies are explicitly tied to this idea, demonstrating coherent focus.  

**Minor limitations.**  The wording âto what extent do media outletsâ¦â is somewhat broad and could have been refined (e.g., specifying a time-frame or type of media more narrowly).  Nevertheless, the topic is consistently and accurately communicated, justifying placement in the 5-6 band, with a score of 5 to recognise the slight scope issue.

**Clear argumentative thread and summative conclusion.**  The student links findings from all three case studies to answer the RQ and reflects on broader diplomatic implications, indicating an attempt at evaluation.  

**Some critical reflection, yet limited balance.**  While the essay notes methodological shortcomings of media outlets, it rarely weighs alternative interpretations of the same texts or reflects on its own analytical limitations, diminishing critical depth.  

**Overall good, not outstanding.**  The discussion moves beyond description but stops short of sustained, nuanced evaluation; hence the upper end of the 4-6 band is justified, with a score of 6.

**Sustained, text-specific analysis.**  Each case study dissects structural and linguistic features (e.g., âEXCLUSIVEâ, passive voice, statistical omission) and links them to constructed reader positions. Quotations are embedded effectively and interpretations are mostly persuasive.  

**Logical progression of ideas.**  The essay moves from micro-level rhetorical choices to macro-level implications, showing an awareness of audience reception and political context.  Conclusions are generally supported by cited evidence.  

**Depth fluctuates.**  Some assertions (e.g., âinevitably hindsighting [sic] them to any sympathyâ) overreach or repeat earlier points, and counter-arguments are marginal.  The overall strength aligns with the lower end of the 7-9 band, warranting a score of 7.

**All required EE sections present.**  The essay includes title page information, abstract equivalent (not mandatory but intro functions), table of contents, clearly labelled chapters, conclusion and bibliography, fulfilling IB structural expectations.  

**Logical flow within and between sections.**  Headings and sub-headings guide the reader; paragraphing is coherent, and transitions generally maintain argumentative momentum.  

**Minor structural inefficiencies.**  HTML-style tags (<b>) occasionally appear in-text, and section numbering resets, but these do not impede comprehension.  Consequently, the work meets âclearly appropriateâ criteria; score 4 within the 3-4 band.

**Demonstrates solid contextual knowledge.**  The introduction outlines historical milestones (Balfour Declaration, Nakba) and integrates contemporary sources (IDF tweet, BBC report, Economist articles), showing clear understanding of both conflict and media studies concepts.  

**Use of sources is generally effective.**  The essay draws on a mixture of primary texts (tweets, news articles, subtitles) and secondary commentary. Citations follow Chicago style and the bibliography is extensive. References are usually integrated to substantiate claims about framing and rhetoric.  

**Some unevenness prevents excellence.**  Analysis leans heavily on ideologically aligned outlets (e.g., Al Jazeera) without systematically addressing counter-perspectives, and a few historical assertions are presented uncritically. These limitations keep the work within the competent 3-4 band; a score of 4 is appropriate.

**Consistent employment of literary and rhetorical terminology.**  The student accurately refers to techniques such as hyperbole, diction, tone, passive/active voice, appeals to authority, and emotional rhetoric, applying them directly to textual examples.  

**Largely accurate, with occasional over-extension.**  While terms are mostly used correctly, there are moments where descriptive commentary drifts into opinionated language, blurring the analytic function of the terminology (e.g., labelling choices as âstrategically manipulativeâ without demonstrating intent).  

**Competent but not exemplary.**  The frequency and reasonable accuracy justify placement in the 3-4 band; greater precision and theoretical grounding would be required for consistent 5-6 performance.  A score of 4 reflects this.

**Stated and mostly focused research question.**  The RQ is explicitly presented at the start and revisited in each section, ensuring a line of inquiry is visible.  

**Partial narrowing and operationalisation.**  Although the student identifies three concrete case studies, the phrase âto what extent do media outletsâ remains wide; key parameters (time period, definition of âmedia outletsâ, criteria for measuring âpromoteâ) are only implicitly, not systematically, defined.  This limits precision and occasionally leads to descriptive rather than investigatory commentary.  

**Overall adequacy with room for sharper focus.**  The connection between the RQ, the selected material and ensuing discussion is generally clear, but the incomplete delineation of scope and evaluative criteria aligns best with the upper end of the 3-4 band; a score of 4 is awarded.

annotations.comment: Incorporate quantitative measures (e.g., frequency of subtitle errors across broadcasts) to substantiate claims of systemic mistranslation bias rather than isolated examples.
The caption notes âScreenshot taken May 2024â but omits a precise day; include full dates to enhance reproducibility and source tracking.
The student persuasively demonstrates the IDFâs misreading of Arabic script, illustrating how linguistic inaccuracies critically undermine the narrativeâs credibility.
Analysis would benefit from considering counterarguments, such as official IDF statements, to deepen evaluation and avoid a one-sided critique.
Periodic reminders of how each rhetorical finding ties back to âto what extentâ the media promotes Palestinians as aggressors would further strengthen the essayâs coherence.
The Washington Post citation lacks a page or section reference; include fuller bibliographic details to adhere to Chicago style precision requirements.
Claiming uniform Palestinian support for Hamas overlooks individual variation; include demographic or survey data to nuance this sweeping generalization.
Social media citations omit publication dates; include full timestamps for tweets to adhere to Chicago style and enable precise source verification.
Figure captions alternate between italic and plain text. Adopt a consistent style for all captions to enhance professional polish.
The discussion of emotive hyperbole and rhythm demonstrates precise application of rhetorical terminology, enhancing analytical clarity and depth.
Labelling Case Study B with a clear header sustains structural coherence and primes the reader for a new analytical focus.
Including the word count at the start of the document precisely adheres to IB formatting guidelines, ensuring transparency and compliance with the 4000-word limit requirement.
The table of contents lists all required sections with clear page ranges, guiding the reader effectively and satisfying structural expectations.
The introduction effectively frames the investigation by linking historical media portrayal to personal experience, engaging the reader and establishing clear purpose and context.
Excessive use of âIbid.â without additional details makes the bibliography harder to navigate; include complete citations for repeated references to improve clarity.
The studentâs clear, concise title effectively communicates the investigationâs focus and meets the IB requirement for a titled investigation.
The presence of raw HTML tags (e.g., <b>) in the table of contents detracts from professional polish; remove these artifacts to improve clarity and readability.
The extended discussion of 9/11âs media impact, while evocative, is only tangentially related to the research question; focus more directly on Western mediaâs portrayal of Palestinians.
Footnote URLs exceed standard length without truncation, disrupting layout; apply Chicago style by abbreviating or using stable DOIs where possible.
The historical overview of the Balfour Declaration and Nakba lacks explicit linkage to how specific media narratives shaped Western perceptions; tie these events directly to your media analysis.
The studentâs use of reputable sources like Al Jazeera and Wavell Room demonstrates solid engagement with diverse perspectives to support the historical context.
The heading for Case Study A clearly labels the section and aligns with IB structural requirements, guiding the reader into the first empirical analysis.
Incorporating the IDF tweet screenshot as a central artefact provides compelling primary evidence, strengthening the analysis of media framing techniques.
The discussion of victim narrative construction through repetition demonstrates effective use of rhetorical terminology and enriches analytical depth.
Explain the criteria for selecting specific utterances in the translation table to justify their representativeness in illustrating BBCâs mistranslation patterns.
The analysis of BBCâs institutional authority and its influence on Western audience trust adds valuable depth, linking editorial reputation to potential bias.
The methodological approach to rhetorical analysis is underexplained; outline specific analytical steps and selection criteria to enhance transparency and replicability.
Accurate attribution of critical counterarguments via FRANCE 24 English shows thorough engagement with diverse media for a balanced case study analysis.
Insightful examination of âEXCLUSIVEâ and âRAWâ effectively unpacks how lexical framing constructs urgency and trust, adding depth to the rhetorical analysis.
The assertion that the tweet âblurs the line between propaganda and factual newsâ requires theoretical backing or empirical evidence; consider citing media-bias frameworks.
The comparative table of correct versus BBCâs erroneous translations offers precise, structured data that anchors the studentâs translation analysis.
Incorporating Tharoorâs analysis on the 2006 Palestinian election situates the mistranslation within broader political dynamics, enriching critical evaluation.
The generalization of Palestinian sentiment risks oversimplification; explore intra-societal diversity or dissenting voices to avoid portraying a monolithic perspective.
Ground the voice analysis in translation theory to explain how passive constructions may reflect editorial policy rather than mere stylistic variation.
Reliance on a single social media source limits evidentiary breadth; incorporate additional outlets or scholarly analyses to strengthen source diversity.
The active vs. passive voice comparison effectively reveals how grammatical choices obscure agency, showcasing strong analytical precision.
The introduction to Case Study C is clearly formatted, signposting a new focus and maintaining structural coherence across the report.
One article quantifies casualties while the other omits statistics altogether; include both sets of figures to maintain analytical balance and fairness.
Establish explicit analytical criteria (e.g., tone, diction, evidence) for comparing the two Economist articles to ensure consistent, systematic evaluation.
The student consistently employs correct heading hierarchy for article excerpts, bolstering the organized presentation of multiple source analyses.
The credit âIMAGE: AFPâ is too minimal; include photographer name and full context to meet academic attribution standards.
Juxtaposing images from both articles offers a compelling visual contrast that underscores differing narratives and enhances comparative depth.
